Keywords: one pager, short, quick, simple, small feature, internal, minor
Use When: Small features, internal tools, minor enhancements, team-level decisions. No cross-team dependencies. Dev time < 1 week.
Required Sections: Overview, Problem, User Stories (2-3), Out of Scope, Success Metric
Markdown Template:
# PRD: [Feature Name]
**Author:** [Name] | **Date:** [Date] | **Status:** Draft

## Problem
[1-2 sentences: What pain exists and evidence that it matters]

## Solution
[1 sentence: What we are building to fix it]

## User Stories
- As a [persona], I want [action] so that [outcome]. **AC:** [1-3 bullet criteria]

## Out of Scope
- [Item]

## Success Metric
[Metric] from [baseline] to [target] within [timeframe]
Notes: Best for: Internal tools, bug-to-feature improvements, fast-follow enhancements after a larger release.

Keywords: full, feature, standard, complete, medium, cross-team, b2b, saas
Use When: Standard feature development with cross-functional teams. 1-4 weeks of engineering. Multiple user stories. Needs stakeholder alignment.
Required Sections: Overview, Problem Statement, Target Users, Goals & Success Metrics, Guardrail Metrics, User Stories (AC required), Functional Requirements, Non-Functional Requirements, Out of Scope, Edge Cases, Technical Considerations, Dependencies, Launch & Rollout, Open Questions

Keywords: epic, initiative, theme, big, large, multi-sprint, strategic
Use When: Large initiatives spanning multiple sprints or teams. Used to align leadership before decomposing into individual feature PRDs.
Required Sections: Overview, Strategic Fit, Success Metrics, Scope (what's in / out), High-Level User Journeys, Dependencies, Milestones
Markdown Template:

**Author:** [Name] | **Date:** [Date] | **Horizon:** Now / Next / Later

Keywords: mvp, minimum viable, launch, v1, first version, startup, validate
Use When: Launching a net-new product or a major new area. Focus on the minimum to test the core hypothesis. Time-boxed scope.
Required Sections: Problem, Target Users, Core Hypothesis, MVP Scope (explicit must-haves), Out of Scope (explicit won't-haves), Success Criteria (validation metrics), Risks
Markdown Template:
# MVP Spec: [Product / Feature Name]
**Author:** [Name] | **Date:** [Date] | **Launch Target:** [Date]

## Core Hypothesis
**We believe** [user type] **has a problem with** [pain point].
**We will solve it by** [proposed solution].
**We'll know this is true when** [measurable outcome].

## Target Users
[Specific persona — 1 paragraph]

## MVP Scope
### Must Have (launch blockers)
- [Feature / capability]

### Won't Have (V1)
- [Feature]
- [Feature]

## Success Criteria (Validation)
| Hypothesis | Metric | Target | Timeline |
|------------|--------|--------|----------|
| Users have this pain | [Metric] | [Goal] | 30 days |

## Risks
| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|------------|
| [Risk] | [Mitigation] |
Notes: The MVP Spec is about learning, not features. Every item in MVP Scope should be tied to the hypothesis being tested.

Keywords: rfc, technical, api, infrastructure, platform, breaking change, migration, architecture
Use When: Features with significant technical decisions that need cross-team alignment before implementation. API changes, data model changes, platform migrations.
Required Sections: Context & Motivation, Problem, Proposed Solution, API / Data Model Changes, Migration Plan, Alternatives Considered, Rollback Plan, Reviewers
Optional Sections: Performance Impact, Security Review, Open Questions
Markdown Template:
# RFC: [Feature/Change Name]
**Author:** [Name] | **Date:** [Date] | **Status:** Draft → Review → Accepted
**Reviewers:** [Engineering leads, Security, Data]

## Context & Motivation
[Why this change is being proposed and what problem it solves]

## Problem
[Technical or product problem that necessitates this RFC]

## Proposed Solution
[Description of the approach]

## API Changes
- New: `POST /api/[endpoint]` — [Description]
- Modified: `GET /api/[endpoint]` — [What changes and backward compatibility]
- Deprecated: `GET /api/[old]` — [Timeline for removal]

## Data Model Changes
[Schema changes, migrations required, data backfill plan]

## Migration Plan
| Step | Owner | Timeline |
|------|-------|----------|
| [Step] | [Team] | [Date] |

## Alternatives Considered
| Option | Pros | Cons | Decision |
|--------|------|------|----------|
| [Alt 1] | | | Rejected: [why] |
| Chosen approach | | | Accepted |

## Rollback Plan
[How to revert if the change causes issues]

## Open Questions
| Question | Owner | Status |
Notes: RFCs are owned by engineering leads but require PM context on user and business impact. PMs provide the problem statement and success criteria.

Keywords: ai, ml, llm, machine learning, model, prediction, generation, suggestion, intelligent, smart
Use When: Any feature involving AI/ML: generative content, predictions, recommendations, intelligent automation. Requires special treatment for model behavior and fallbacks.
Required Sections: Problem & Use Case, User Intent, Desired AI Behavior, Input/Output Spec, Confidence & Fallback, Edge Cases (AI-specific), User Control & Override, Privacy & Data Usage, Success Metrics (quality + adoption), Guardrails
Optional Sections: Model Performance Targets, Human-in-the-Loop, Evaluation Plan
Markdown Template:
# AI Feature PRD: [Feature Name]
**Author:** [Name] | **Date:** [Date]

## Problem & Use Case
[Why does this feature need AI? What manual/broken task is it replacing?]

## User Intent
[What is the user trying to accomplish? What does a good AI response look like from the user's perspective?]

## Desired AI Behavior
| Input | Expected Output | Quality Signal |
|-------|-----------------|----------------|
| [User prompt / data] | [What AI should produce] | [How to evaluate] |

## Confidence & Fallback
- **High confidence (>X%):** [Show result with confidence indicator]
- **Low confidence (<X%):** [Show with warning: 'Low confidence — please review']
- **AI unavailable:** [Fallback behavior — e.g., show template or disable feature gracefully]

## User Control & Override
- User can [accept / reject / edit] AI output before it is saved
- AI-generated content is [labeled / not labeled] as AI-generated
- User override is [logged for model improvement / discarded]

## Privacy & Data Usage
- Training data source: [User data / Public data / Partner data]
- PII handling: [Is PII sent to the model? How is it handled?]
- GDPR/compliance: [User right to opt out, data deletion]

## Success Metrics
| Metric | Baseline | Target |
|--------|----------|--------|
| Task completion rate with AI | — | >X% |
| User acceptance rate (AI output kept) | — | >X% |
| Latency (p95) | — | <Xs |
| Override/rejection rate | — | <X% |

## Guardrails
- Output must not [harmful content definition]
- Model must not [specific undesired behavior]
Notes: AI features require an evaluation plan before launch. Define what 'good' looks like for the model and how you'll test it.

Keywords: api, integration, webhook, connect, sync, third-party, oauth, partner
Use When: Building a new API endpoint, adding a third-party integration, or creating a data sync between two systems.
Required Sections: Problem & Developer Persona, Use Case, API Design (endpoints / schema), Authentication, Error Handling, Rate Limits, Developer Experience, Documentation Plan, Success Metrics
Optional Sections: SDK, Webhook support, Migration from old API

Keywords: bug, regression, fix, improvement, polish, technical debt, ux debt
Use When: When a bug report reveals a systemic problem that warrants a proper feature response rather than a point fix. Common for UX debt and recurring support categories.
Required Sections: Problem Origin (bug context), Root Cause Analysis, User Impact, Proposed Solution, Regression Prevention, Success Metric
Markdown Template:
# PRD: [Feature Name] (Bug → Feature)
**Origin:** Bug [TICKET-ID] reported by [Source]
**Author:** [Name] | **Date:** [Date]

## Bug Context
[Brief description of the bug and how it was reported. Link to original ticket.]

## Root Cause
[What caused the bug? Is this a symptom of a deeper UX or technical issue?]

## User Impact
- Frequency: [How often does this occur?]
- Severity: [What does the user experience?]
- Volume: [How many users are affected per week?]

## Proposed Solution
[Description of the proper fix — not just a patch, but a correct behavior]

## Regression Prevention
- [ ] Automated test case added for this scenario
- [ ] QA sign-off on edge cases [list them]

## Success Metric
- [Metric before]: [Current value]
- [Target after fix]: [Goal]
- [How measured]: [Analytics event or support ticket count]
Notes: Use this template when a bug reveals a gap that needs a proper product decision, not just a code patch.

Keywords: micro, ticket, jira, story, small, simple, one story, quick
Use When: Single-story tickets where a full PRD is unnecessary. One user story, one outcome, quick validation.
Required Sections: Story (As a / I want / So that), AC (3-5 items), Out of Scope (1-2 items)
Markdown Template:
## [Story Title]

**As a** [specific persona],
**I want** [clear action],
**so that** [tangible outcome].

**Priority:** P0 / P1 / P2

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] [Criterion 1]
- [ ] [Criterion 2]
- [ ] [Edge case handled]

**Out of Scope:** [What this ticket does NOT include]

**Design:** [Figma link if applicable]
Notes: Use for: fast-follow items after a major release, small UX improvements, copy changes, or single-component changes. If this ticket spawns 3+ stories, write a One-Pager PRD instead.
